function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

     3|# This file is the authoritative definition of the case lifecycle:
     4|# every phase, every landmark, what gates phase transitions, and

     9|# reconciler reads this file plus the vault and computes which
    10|# landmarks are now satisfied. The narrative phase READMEs and
    11|# landmarks.md files are documentation; this YAML is the contract.
    12|#
    13|# Conditions are expressed as small predicates the materializer
    14|# evaluates against a Case object loaded from cases/<slug>/<slug>.md
    15|# per skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md.

    17|# Predicate vocabulary (Python-ish, evaluated with safe_eval):
    18|#   case.status == "intake"                  # frontmatter status
    19|#   case.frontmatter.<key> == <value>        # any frontmatter key
    20|#   case.has_document("hipaa")               # filename pattern in cases/<slug>/documents/
    21|#   case.has_section("Insurance Claims")     # markdown heading present
    22|#   case.section_bullets("Insurance Claims") > 0

    24|#   case.claims.count > 0
    25|#   case.liens.where(status="outstanding").count == 0

    30|#
    31|# Authors: write predicates that touch ONLY the vault. Anything that

    35|schema_version: 1

    37|phases:
    38|
    39|  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    40|  phase_0_onboarding:
    41|    name: Onboarding
    42|    description: >
    43|      Initial client retention. Three signed documents must be on file
    44|      before any substantive work begins.

    53|          - workflow: document_collection
    54|            step: receive_signed_intake
    55|      - id: contract_signed
    56|        name: Contract Signed

    58|        condition: case.has_document("fee agreement")
    59|        produced_by:

    61|            step: receive_signed_contract
    62|      - id: medical_auth_signed
    63|        name: Medical Auth Signed

    65|        condition: case.has_document("hipaa") or case.has_document("medical authorization")
    66|        produced_by:

    93|          - workflow: case_setup
    94|      - id: accident_report_obtained
    95|        name: Accident Report Obtained

    98|        produced_by:
    99|          - workflow: accident_report
   100|      - id: insurance_claims_setup
   101|        name: Insurance Claims Setup

   104|        produced_by:

   121|  phase_2_treatment:

{"content": "     1|# Data Contract — FirmVault Paralegal System
     2|
     3|This document is the **single source of truth** for where each piece of case data lives in the FirmVault Obsidian vault. Every skill, tool, and workflow in this directory MUST read and write case data through these paths. Do not invent new locations.
     4|
     5|> **Important:** The system used to back state in a FalkorDB graph. That is no longer the case. The Obsidian vault is the only state store. References to FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`, or per-case JSON files (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`) are deprecated and being removed.
     6|
     7|## 1. Repository layout
     8|
     9|```
    10|FirmVault/                                  # repo root, also the Obsidian vault root
    11|├── cases/<case-slug>/                      # one folder per matter

    29|## 2. The case file: `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md`
    30|
    31|This is the canonical record for a matter. Every other file in `cases/<slug>/` either renders or extends it.

    54|
    55|## Medical Providers
    56|- [[contacts/<provider-slug>|<Provider Name>]]
    57|<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->

    61|

    74|
    75|## Expenses
    76|| Creditor | Amount | Category |
    77|
    78|## Activity Log
    79|Recent: [[Activity Log/...]]
    80|```
    81|
    82|The `<!-- roscoe-*-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-*-end -->` markers are managed by the import scripts and will be replaced on every sync. **Anything between the markers is overwritten.** Do not edit by hand.

    85|
    86|| Question agents need answered | Read from | Write to |
    87||---|---|---|
    88|| What phase is this case in? | `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` frontmatter `status` | Same |
    89|| What landmarks have been hit? | Frontmatter `landmarks: {…}` (boolean map) | Same |
    90|| Who is the client? | Frontmatter `client_name` + `Contacts/Clients/<slug>.md` | Both |
    91|| What insurance does the case have? | `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` `## Insurance Claims` + `cases/<slug>/claims/*.md` | New file in `claims/` + bullet in case file |
    92|| Who are the adjusters? | `cases/<slug>/contacts/<adjuster-slug>.md` linking to `Contacts/Insurance/<slug>.md` | Same |
    93|| Who are the medical providers? | `cases/<slug>/contacts/<provider-slug>.md` linking to `Contacts/Medical/<slug>.md` | Same |
    94|| What's the treatment status? | `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` table in case file | Replaced wholesale by importer |
    95|| What liens exist? | `cases/<slug>/liens/*.md` + `## Liens` section | New file + bullet |
    96|| What expenses are tracked? | `## Expenses` table in case file | Append row |
    97|| What was logged today? | `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-<category>.md` | New file per event |
    98|| What documents are on file? | `cases/<slug>/documents/` (filesystem listing) | Drop files there |
    99|| What template do I use for X? | `Templates/<slug>.docx` (or `.pdf`) | Read-only |
   100|
   101|## 4. Slug rules
   102|
   103|All slugs throughout the vault follow the same convention:
   104|
   105|- Lowercase
   106|- Apostrophes and curly quotes stripped (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
   107|- `&` → `and`
   108|- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
   109|- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped
   110|
   111|Examples: `Allstate Insurance` → `allstate-insurance`, `K'daya Austin` → `kdaya-austin`, `River City Orthopedics & Sports Medicine` → `river-city-orthopedics-and-sports-medicine`.
   112|
   113|## 5. Activity log entry format
   114|
   115|Filename: `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-<category>[-N].md`. Categories agents may use: `correspondence`, `legal`, `phone`, `meeting`, `imported`, `system`. The `-N` suffix disambiguates multiple entries within the same minute.
   116|
   117|Frontmatter:
   118|
   119|```yaml
   120|---
   121|schema_version: 2
   122|case_id: <uuid>                  # mirrors the parent case file
   123|date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
   124|time: "HH:MM:SS"
   125|category: <category>
   126|subcategory: <free-form>
   127|---
   128|```
   129|
   130|Body always opens with the wikilink back to the case:
   131|
   132|```markdown
   133|# <category> — <date>
   134|
   135|**Case:** [[cases/<slug>/<slug>|<Client Name>]]
   136|
   137|<note body>
   138|```
   139|
   140|## 6. Templates
   141|
   142|The firm template library lives in `Templates/<slug>.{docx,pdf}` at the repo root. Templates are read-only firm assets. Skills that fill templates should:
   143|
   144|1. Read the template from `Templates/<slug>.docx`.
   145|2. Write the filled output to `cases/<slug>/documents/<descriptive-name>.docx`.
   146|3. Never modify the source template.
   147|
   148|A template index lives at `Templates/INDEX.md` (to be created in a follow-up commit) listing each template, its purpose, and the placeholders it expects.
   149|
   150|## 7. What is NOT in the vault
   151|
   152|To keep the contract honest, the following are explicitly **not** stored in the vault and skills should not look for them:
   153|
   154|- Per-case JSON state files (`overview.json`, etc.) — deleted, do not recreate
   155|- A FalkorDB graph instance — removed
   156|- A `${ROSCOE_ROOT}` environment variable — removed; everything is repo-relative
   157|- Proprietary Filevine internal IDs beyond what's already imported into activity log notes
   158|- Scanned medical records, billing PDFs from providers — those go in `cases/<slug>/documents/`, not in the vault metadata
   159|
   160|## 8. Reading and writing rules for agents
   161|
   162|1. **Reads** are cheap. Always read `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` first to ground yourself in the case before any action.
   163|2. **Writes** must preserve frontmatter and section ordering. When in doubt, edit existing sections rather than appending new ones.
   164|3. **Never** edit content between `<!-- roscoe-*-start -->` and `<!-- roscoe-*-end -->` markers — those get overwritten by syncs.
   165|4. **Slugify** any new filename using the rules in §4. Mismatched slugs break wikilinks.
   166|5. **Wikilinks** use the form `[[cases/<slug>/<slug>|<Display Name>]]` for case files, `[[contacts/<slug>|<Display Name>]]` for per-case contact stubs, `[[Contacts/Insurance/<slug>|<Display Name>]]` for master cards.
   167|6. **Commits** should describe the agent action and the case affected. The git history is part of the audit trail.
   168|
   169|## 9. When this contract changes
   170|
   171|Any change to vault layout, frontmatter schemas, or section conventions MUST be reflected here in the same commit that introduces the change. Agents and skills consult this file as authoritative; drift between this doc and reality is the bug.

    81|### 1.3 The firmvault contract (what "contract layer" means)
    82|
    83|firmvault is the **shared reference surface** both tracks consume. It is not itself a runtime. Its job is to define, in a runtime-agnostic way:
    84|
    85|1. **Where case state lives** — the filesystem layout of `cases/<slug>/` and `Contacts/`, the frontmatter schemas, the slug rules, the section conventions. Canonical source: `skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md`.
    86|2. **The phase state machine** — which landmarks exist per phase, which predicates (evaluated against the vault) determine whether a landmark is satisfied, which workflows produce which landmarks, how phases transition. Canonical source: `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml`.
    87|3. **The skill library** — 42 skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format, progressively disclosed via `references/*.md`. Each skill names the PHASE_DAG landmark it produces. Canonical source: `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/<slug>/SKILL.md`.
    88|4. **The task queue contract** — what a task issue looks like (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle), how tasks materialize from landmark gaps, how workers pick them up. Canonical source: `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md`.
    89|5. **The firm template library** — 96 firm forms (intake, LOR, complaints, lien letters, etc.) with slug filenames and an `INDEX.md` catalog. Canonical source: `Templates/`.

   122|## Phase 2 — Canonical flow + the Track A bake-off
   123|
   124|Phase 2 was originally going to walk three end-to-end scenarios (intake, worker-drafts-LOR, secrets-reveal). The scope narrowed after Phase 1 questions were answered:
   125|
   126|- Intake is **Track B only** (Mailroom Watcher). Track A is scoped to the already-imported cases (Q4 answered "a").
   127|- Secrets reveal is **design-only** for now. No implementation in either track yet. Deferred to Phase 3.
   128|- That leaves **one canonical flow** to trace for Track A: a case has a landmark gap → that gap becomes an actionable unit of work → an agent does the work → a human reviews → the vault is updated → the landmark flips.
   129|
   130|Rather than tracing that flow through one concrete architecture, Phase 2 traces it through **five candidate architectures in parallel** — the Track A bake-off. Each is a distinct combination of task store + dispatcher + UI + review gate. Each is scaffolded under `experiments/arch-<n>-<name>/`. This section describes the abstract flow, the five concrete instantiations, the comparison dimensions, and the recommendation for which to pilot first.
   131|
   132|### 2.1 The abstract flow (roles any architecture must fill)
   133|
   134|Independent of runtime, the Track A loop requires these seven roles. Any architecture is just a different assignment of components to these roles.
   135|
   136|| Role | What it does |
   137||---|---|
   138|| **Case watcher / materializer** | Walks the vault on a cadence, evaluates landmark predicates against case frontmatter, identifies gaps, emits new work units. Reads vault. Idempotent. |
   139|| **Task store** | Holds the queue of work units. Supports `list`, `create`, `update status`, `get by id`. Must be observable by a human. |
   140|| **Dispatcher** | Picks a ready task, binds it to an agent (Claude Code / Codex / Gemini CLI), hands off execution. Agent-agnostic by requirement. |
   141|| **Runner** | The environment where the agent actually executes. Has access to a git worktree of the vault, credentials for the chosen CLI, and write access to produce artifacts. |
   142|| **Artifact writer** | Commits the agent's output to the vault (case file edits, new documents) on a task-specific branch. Never writes directly to main. |
   143|| **Review gate** | A human checkpoint. Sees the diff (or a summary of it), approves or rejects with feedback. Approval is what lets the work land. |
   144|| **Landmark updater** | After merge, re-evaluates the task's `success_check` against the post-merge vault. If satisfied, flips the case's `landmarks.<id>` frontmatter to `true` and closes the task. |
   145|
   146|Outside the seven-role loop there are two supporting roles worth naming:
   147|
   148|- **Reconciler** — re-evaluates downstream landmark dependencies and materializes the next wave of tasks. Typically the next materializer tick does this job.
   149|- **UI / dashboard** — gives the operator visibility into queue depth, in-flight tasks, failures, cost. Track A requires this as a hard success criterion.
   150|
   151|### 2.2 The five candidate architectures
   152|
   153|Each architecture is a scaffolded reference prototype under `experiments/`. None of them have been deployed yet. Each was built by a parallel subagent following a self-contained brief.

   155|| # | Architecture | `experiments/` path | Files / lines |

   158|| **2** | **Vibe Kanban** (REST API + per-task worktrees + diff review) | `arch-2-vibe-kanban/` | 5 / ~1,290 |
   159|| **3** | **Mission Control** (SQLite + Aegis reviewer + queue-poll dispatch) | `arch-3-mission-control/` | 6 / ~1,070 |
   160|| **4** | **Custom Case Cockpit** (Next.js + Drizzle + Postgres + case-centric UI) | `arch-4-case-cockpit/` | 28 / ~2,076 |

   162|
   163|### 2.3 Role-by-architecture mapping
   164|
   165|Same seven roles, five architectures. Read a column to see how one architecture assigns components to roles; read a row to see how the same role is implemented differently across architectures.
   166|
   167|| Role | Arch 1 Pure GitHub | Arch 2 Vibe Kanban | Arch 3 Mission Control | Arch 4 Case Cockpit | Arch 5 schedule skill |
   168||---|---|---|---|---|---|
   169|| Materializer | `gh aw` cron workflow (`materializer.md`) | `materializer.py` stdlib script (cron'd on Render or locally) | `materializer.py` stdlib + PyYAML (cron in MC blueprint every 10m) | `src/lib/materializer.ts` + `/api/materialize` route (manual or cron) | Scheduled Claude agent running `materializer-prompt.md` |
   170|| Task store | GitHub Issues | Vibe Kanban's Rust backend (workspace records) | MC's SQLite (`tasks` table) | Postgres via Drizzle (`tasks` table) | GitHub Issues |
   171|| Dispatcher | `gh aw` worker workflow, bash `case` on CLI | VK native (bundled executor) | Queue-poll via `worker-shell-hook.sh` | `src/lib/worker.ts` via `child_process.spawn` | Scheduled Claude agent running `worker-prompt.md` |
   172|| Runner | GitHub Actions runner (shells out to `claude` / `codex` / `gemini`) | VK-managed git worktree in its container | Host running the shell hook (wherever that is) | Next.js server runtime (wherever the app runs) | Claude Code's scheduled-agent runtime (likely managed by Anthropic) |
   173|| Artifact writer | `git commit` + `gh pr create` in runner | VK commits inside worktree, diff review UI | `git` + `gh pr create` from the shell hook | `simple-git` calls from `worker.ts` | `git commit` + `gh pr create` inside the scheduled agent |
   174|| Review gate | GitHub PR review | VK's diff view + merge button (can also open GH PR) | Aegis auto-reviewer **then** GitHub PR review | GitHub PR (or dashboard button, MVP = PR) | GitHub PR review |
   175|| Landmark updater | `gh aw` workflow on `pull_request.closed` (`landmark-detector.md`) | Post-merge git hook or polling materializer | Next materializer tick | Next materializer tick | Scheduled Claude agent running `landmark-detector-prompt.md` |
   176|
   177|### 2.4 Comparison dimensions
   178|
   179|The bake-off is trying to answer specific questions. These are the dimensions I'll use to score each arch after it runs.
   180|
   181|| Dimension | Arch 1 | Arch 2 | Arch 3 | Arch 4 | Arch 5 |
   182||---|---|---|---|---|---|
   183|| Infra to deploy | None | One Render service | One Render service | One web service + managed Postgres | None |
   184|| Multi-agent dispatch (hard requirement) | ✅ Bash shell-out to all three CLIs | ✅ Native | ⚠ Queue-poll + shell hook (works, non-trivial) | ✅ `child_process.spawn` | ❌ Very likely Claude-only |
   185|| Review gate | GitHub PR (strong) | VK diff view (strong) | Aegis LLM review (weak — narrative only) + PR (strong) | GitHub PR (strong) | GitHub PR (strong) |
   186|| Observability / UI | GitHub Projects v2 (functional, not great) | VK kanban (good) | MC's 32 panels (strongest) | Custom case-centric pages (tailored) | GitHub Issues only (weakest) |
   187|| Setup friction | Low: install `gh aw`, compile, set secrets | Medium: deploy VK, configure API key, register repo | Medium-high: deploy MC, register agents, configure Aegis SOUL | High: deploy app + DB, configure env vars | Low: three scheduled commands |
   188|| Time to first green run | ~30 min | ~2 hours | ~3 hours | ~4 hours | ~15 min |
   189|| Runtime cost per month (steady state) | $0 + Actions minutes + agent tokens | ~$7 Render Standard + agent tokens | ~$7 Render Starter + agent tokens | ~$14 Render (app + DB) + agent tokens | $0 + agent tokens (likely higher — polling burns) |
   190|| Agent lock-in risk | Low (any CLI) | Low (any CLI VK supports) | Medium (MC dispatcher surface is narrower) | Low (any CLI you can spawn) | **High** (Claude only) |
   191|| Vault source-of-truth purity | ✅ Strict | ⚠ VK holds some metadata alongside | ⚠ MC holds task + audit in SQLite | ⚠ Postgres holds queue + audit | ✅ Strict |
   192|| Fits "firm as code repo" thesis | 10/10 | 6/10 | 5/10 | 6/10 | 9/10 |
   193|| Fits "multi-experiment testing orchestration pattern" goal | 8/10 | 7/10 | 9/10 | 5/10 | 6/10 |
   194|
   195|Scores are opinionated, not empirical. The pilot will produce evidence.
   196|
   197|### 2.5 Architecture-specific findings from the build
   198|
   199|Things the subagents discovered while building, worth remembering before the pilot:
   200|
   201|- **Arch 1**: `gh aw`'s exact frontmatter schema (`engine`, `tools`, `with`, `concurrency`) is not fully verified. First `gh aw compile` run will probably surface schema errors that need fixing. CLI flags for `claude --print` / `codex exec` / `gemini --prompt` are plausible guesses that will also need tweaking once something actually runs.
   202|- **Arch 2**: Vibe Kanban's real API endpoint is **`POST /api/workspaces/start`**, not `/api/tasks` — `/api/tasks` was deprecated/renamed. Verified by reading the VK Rust source (`crates/server/src/routes/workspaces/mod.rs`). The subagent corrected the materializer to use the right endpoint. Seven other uncertainties about VK's internals are flagged in `experiments/arch-2-vibe-kanban/firmvault-vk-integration.md §6`.
   203|- **Arch 3**: Mission Control has **no `/api/tasks/<id>/resolution` endpoint** despite what the brief assumed. Resolutions are written via `PUT /api/tasks/<id>` with `{resolution, status}`. Verified from MC source. Also: MC's Aegis reviewer **only sees the first 6000 chars of the worker's resolution text, not the diff** — which is a significant weakness for paralegal work where the artifact is the deliverable.
   204|- **Arch 4**: The worker has a **deterministic fallback path for `write-case-summary`** — the bake-off smoke test passes even without an agent CLI installed, which lets you prove the architecture works end-to-end without burning API tokens. Clever touch.
   205|- **Arch 5**: The subagent was **honest about this architecture's weaknesses** — `schedule` is almost certainly Claude-only (failing Track A criterion #1), has the weakest observability, burns the most tokens per unit of useful work (polling, no event-drivenness), creates agent lock-in, and has no webhook story for the landmark detector. Its only honest win is "speed to first working loop" — a legitimate reason to build it as a baseline before porting prompts into a stronger architecture.
   206|
   207|### 2.6 Recommendation: which to pilot first, and in what order
   208|
   209|**Pilot order**: 1 → 2, then decide.
   210|
   211|**Arch 1 first** because:
   212|
   213|- Zero deployment cost — everything lives inside the repo and GitHub
   214|- Fastest way to validate whether the `write-case-summary` test harness actually works end-to-end with a real agent
   215|- Tests the critical `gh aw` multi-agent dispatch assumption that everything else is trying to work around
   216|- If Arch 1 works, it's likely "good enough" for the orchestration-pattern test the user mentioned — most of the wanted features are already there
   217|- If Arch 1 fails, the failure teaches us exactly what Arch 2/3/4 need to replace
   218|
   219|**Arch 2 second** because:
   220|
   221|- If Arch 1 works, Arch 2 is the obvious UX upgrade — native multi-agent in worktrees plus a real diff review experience is a stronger product story than GitHub Projects
   222|- One deployment on Render — small but real infra cost, worth testing separately
   223|- Exercises the "the materializer is a thin bridge, the UI is someone else's code" hypothesis, which is worth knowing for the product pitch
   224|- The subagent verified the VK API shape against source, so the materializer should work on first run
   225|
   226|**Arch 3 (Mission Control) third**, conditionally: pilot only if Arch 1 and Arch 2 both succeed AND we want to evaluate MC's observability panels and Aegis gate as additive layers, not as the primary runtime. Mission Control's strongest pitch is the dashboard, not the dispatcher.
   227|
   228|**Arch 4 (Custom Case Cockpit) fourth**, conditionally: pilot only if none of Arch 1/2/3 deliver a good case-centric UI experience. Building a custom UI has significant opportunity cost vs. using one of the existing tools.
   229|
   230|**Arch 5 (schedule skill) as baseline**: build and smoke-test it **alongside Arch 1** because it's effectively free — same 15-minute setup, same task template, same GitHub Issues store, just different dispatch and cadence. If Arch 5 fires correctly and Arch 1 doesn't, we have a fallback. If both work, we have a concrete comparison between `gh aw` and `schedule` as schedulers.

{"content": "     1|"""GSD (Get Shit Done) adapter — scans GSD project workspaces and drives
     2|the plan-parse → dispatch → state-update lifecycle from the daemon heartbeat.
     3|
     4|Unlike the other adapters that talk to remote APIs, this one shells out to
     5|the Node.js GSD library (gsd-lawyerinc) which lives on disk.  The daemon
     6|calls into it on each tick to:
     7|
     8|  1. Discover active projects (dirs with .planning/STATE.md).
     9|  2. Read state to find which wave/phase is current.
    10|  3. Check if the current wave is complete.
    11|  4. Dispatch the next wave if ready (via GSD's dispatcher).
    12|  5. Update STATE.md with results.
    13|  6. Flag tasks that need Aaron's approval for Telegram notification.
    14|
    15|Environment variables:
    16|  GSD_PROJECTS_DIR — root directory containing GSD project workspaces.
    17|                     Default: ~/projects  (or /opt/data/projects on Railway)
    18|  GSD_PACKAGE_DIR  — path to the gsd-lawyerinc package.
    19|                     Default: /opt/data/gsd-lawyerinc
    20|"""
    21|
    22|from __future__ import annotations
    23|
    24|import json
    25|import logging
    26|import os
    27|import subprocess
    28|from dataclasses import dataclass, field
    29|from pathlib import Path
    30|from typing import Optional
    31|
    32|from daemon.adapters.base import (
    33|    HealthCheckable,
    34|    HealthStatus,
    35|    Task,
    36|    TaskSource,
    37|    TaskStatus,
    38|)
    39|
    40|logger = logging.getLogger("daemon.gsd")
    41|
    42|
    43|@dataclass
    44|class GSDProject:
    45|    """Represents a discovered GSD project workspace."""
    46|    name: str
    47|    path: Path
    48|    lifecycle: str = ""
    49|    phase: str = ""
    50|    wave: int = 0
    51|    tasks_total: int = 0
    52|    tasks_done: int = 0
    53|    needs_approval: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    54|    wave_complete: bool = False
    55|
    56|
    57|class GSDAdapter(TaskSource, HealthCheckable):
    58|    """Adapter that drives GSD project lifecycle from the daemon heartbeat."""
    59|
    60|    def __init__(
    61|        self,
    62|        projects_dir: Optional[str] = None,
    63|        package_dir: Optional[str] = None,
    64|    ) -> None:
    65|        self.projects_dir = Path(
    66|            projects_dir
    67|            or os.environ.get("GSD_PROJECTS_DIR", "/opt/data/projects")
    68|        )
    69|        self.package_dir = Path(
    70|            package_dir
    71|            or os.environ.get("GSD_PACKAGE_DIR", "/opt/data/gsd-lawyerinc")
    72|        )
    73|        # Track which projects we've already dispatched for this tick
    74|        # to avoid double-dispatching
    75|        self._last_dispatch: dict[str, int] = {}  # project_name -> wave
    76|
    77|    @property
    78|    def configured(self) -> bool:
    79|        """True if GSD package and at least one project directory exist."""
    80|        return self.package_dir.exists() and self.projects_dir.exists()
    81|
    82|    # ── HealthCheckable ────────────────────────────────────────────────
    83|
    84|    async def health_check(self) -> HealthStatus:
    85|        if not self.configured:
    86|            return HealthStatus(
    87|                service_name="gsd",
    88|                healthy=False,
    89|                latency_ms=0,
    90|                error="GSD not configured — package or projects dir missing",
    91|            )
    92|
    93|        # Quick check: can we run the GSD node script?
    94|        try:
    95|            result = self._run_gsd_script("health_check", timeout=5)
    96|            return HealthStatus(
    97|                service_name="gsd",
    98|                healthy=result.get("ok", False),
    99|                latency_ms=result.get("latency_ms", 0),
   100|                error=result.get("error"),
   101|            )
   102|        except Exception as exc:
   103|            return HealthStatus(
   104|                service_name="gsd",
   105|                healthy=False,
   106|                latency_ms=0,
   107|                error=str(exc),
   108|            )
   109|
   110|    # ── TaskSource ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   111|
   112|    async def poll_tasks(self) -> list[Task]:
   113|        """Scan GSD projects for tasks ready to dispatch.
   114|
   115|        This is called on every daemon tick.  It:
   116|          1. Scans projects_dir for .planning/STATE.md dirs.
   117|          2. For each active project (lifecycle == EXECUTE), reads the state.
   118|          3. If the current wave is complete, returns tasks from the next wave
   119|             as Task objects for the daemon to process.
   120|        """
   121|        if not self.configured:
   122|            return []
   123|
   124|        tasks: list[Task] = []
   125|
   126|        try:
   127|            projects = self._discover_projects()
   128|        except Exception as exc:
   129|            logger.error("gsd: error discovering projects — %s", exc)
   130|            return []
   131|
   132|        for project in projects:
   133|            try:
   134|                project_tasks = await self._poll_project(project)
   135|                tasks.extend(project_tasks)
   136|            except Exception as exc:
   137|                logger.error(
   138|                    "gsd: error polling project %s — %s", project.name, exc
   139|                )
   140|
   141|        return tasks
   142|
   143|    async def update_task_status(
   144|        self, task_id: str, status: TaskStatus
   145|    ) -> bool:
   146|        """Update a GSD task's status in STATE.md."""
   147|        # task_id format: "project_name:TASK-ID"
   148|        parts = task_id.split(":", 1)
   149|        if len(parts) != 2:
   150|            logger.warning("gsd: invalid task_id format: %s", task_id)
   151|            return False
   152|
   153|        project_name, gsd_task_id = parts
   154|
   155|        gsd_status_map = {
   156|            TaskStatus.PENDING: "planned",
   157|            TaskStatus.ASSIGNED: "dispatched",
   158|            TaskStatus.IN_PROGRESS: "in_progress",
   159|            TaskStatus.PENDING_REVIEW: "reviewing",
   160|            TaskStatus.APPROVED: "done",
   161|            TaskStatus.REJECTED: "blocked",
   162|            TaskStatus.ERROR: "blocked",
   163|        }
   164|
   165|        gsd_status = gsd_status_map.get(status, "planned")
   166|
   167|        try:
   168|            result = self._run_gsd_script("update_status", {
   169|                "project": project_name,
   170|                "taskId": gsd_task_id,
   171|                "status": gsd_status,
   172|            })
   173|            return result.get("ok", False)
   174|        except Exception as exc:
   175|            logger.error(
   176|                "gsd: error updating status for %s — %s", task_id, exc
   177|            )
   178|            return False
   179|
   180|    # ── Internal ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   181|
   182|    def _discover_projects(self) -> list[GSDProject]:
   183|        """Find all directories under projects_dir that have .planning/STATE.md."""
   184|        projects = []
   185|        if not self.projects_dir.exists():
   186|            return projects
   187|
   188|        for entry in sorted(self.projects_dir.iterdir()):
   189|            state_file = entry / ".planning" / "STATE.md"
   190|            if entry.is_dir() and state_file.exists():
   191|                projects.append(GSDProject(name=entry.name, path=entry))
   192|
   193|        return projects
   194|
   195|    async def _poll_project(self, project: GSDProject) -> list[Task]:
   196|        """Check a single project and return dispatchable tasks."""
   197|        result = self._run_gsd_script("poll_project", {
   198|            "project": project.name,
   199|            "projectPath": str(project.path),
   200|        })
   201|
   202|        if not result.get("ok"):
   203|            if result.get("lifecycle") not in ("EXECUTE",):
   204|                logger.debug(
   205|                    "gsd: project %s lifecycle=%s — skipping",
   206|                    project.name,
   207|                    result.get("lifecycle", "unknown"),
   208|                )
   209|            else:
   210|                logger.warning(
   211|                    "gsd: poll failed for %s — %s",
   212|                    project.name,
   213|                    result.get("error", "unknown"),
   214|                )
   215|            return []
   216|
   217|        action = result.get("action")
   218|        tasks: list[Task] = []
   219|
   220|        if action == "dispatch_wave":
   221|            wave_tasks = result.get("tasks", [])
   222|            wave_num = result.get("wave", 0)
   223|            logger.info(
   224|                "gsd: project %s — dispatching wave %d (%d tasks)",
   225|                project.name, wave_num, len(wave_tasks),
   226|            )
   227|            for t in wave_tasks:
   228|                tasks.append(Task(
   229|                    id=f"{project.name}:{t['id']}",
   230|                    type=t.get("type", "general"),
   231|                    payload=json.dumps(t),
   232|                    metadata={
   233|                        "source": "gsd",
   234|                        "project": project.name,
   235|                        "wave": wave_num,
   236|                        "assignee": t.get("assignee", ""),
   237|                        "approval": t.get("approval", ""),
   238|                        "gsd_task": t,
   239|                    },
   240|                    status=TaskStatus.PENDING,
   241|                ))
   242|
   243|        elif action == "needs_approval":
   244|            approval_tasks = result.get("tasks", [])
   245|            logger.info(
   246|                "gsd: project %s — %d task(s) awaiting Aaron's approval",
   247|                project.name, len(approval_tasks),
   248|            )
   249|            for t in approval_tasks:
   250|                tasks.append(Task(
   251|                    id=f"{project.name}:{t['id']}",
   252|                    type="approval",
   253|                    payload=json.dumps(t),
   254|                    metadata={
   255|                        "source": "gsd",
   256|                        "project": project.name,
   257|                        "approval": "aaron",
   258|                        "gsd_task": t,
   259|                    },
   260|                    status=TaskStatus.PENDING_REVIEW,
   261|                ))
   262|
   263|        elif action == "advance_lifecycle":
   264|            next_lifecycle = result.get("next_lifecycle", "VERIFY")
   265|            logger.info(
   266|                "gsd: project %s — all waves complete, advancing to %s",
   267|                project.name, next_lifecycle,
   268|            )
   269|
   270|        elif action == "idle":
   271|            logger.debug("gsd: project %s — idle, nothing to dispatch", project.name)
   272|
   273|        return tasks
   274|
   275|    def _run_gsd_script(
   276|        self,
   277|        command: str,
   278|        args: Optional[dict] = None,
   279|        timeout: int = 30,
   280|    ) -> dict:
   281|        """Run the GSD bridge script and return parsed JSON output.
   282|
   283|        The bridge script (daemon/gsd_bridge.js) is a thin Node.js wrapper
   284|        that imports gsd-lawyerinc and exposes its functions as CLI commands
   285|        with JSON I/O.
   286|        """
   287|        bridge_path = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "gsd_bridge.mjs"
   288|        if not bridge_path.exists():
   289|            return {"ok": False, "error": f"GSD bridge not found at {bridge_path}"}
   290|
   291|        cmd = ["node", str(bridge_path), command]
   292|        input_data = json.dumps(args) if args else "{}"
   293|
   294|        try:
   295|            proc = subprocess.run(
   296|                cmd,
   297|                input=input_data,
   298|                capture_output=True,
   299|                text=True,
   300|                timeout=timeout,
   301|                cwd=str(self.projects_dir),
   302|                env={
   303|                    **os.environ,
   304|                    "GSD_PROJECTS_DIR": str(self.projects_dir),
   305|                    "GSD_PACKAGE_DIR": str(self.package_dir),
   306|                },
   307|            )
   308|
   309|            if proc.returncode != 0:
   310|                stderr = proc.stderr.strip()
   311|                logger.warning("gsd bridge error: %s", stderr[:500])
   312|                return {"ok": False, "error": stderr[:500]}
   313|
   314|            output = proc.stdout.strip()
   315|            if not output:
   316|                return {"ok": False, "error": "empty output from bridge"}
   317|
   318|            return json.loads(output)
   319|
   320|        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
   321|            return {"ok": False, "error": f"GSD bridge timed out ({timeout}s)"}
   322|        except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
   323|            return {"ok": False, "error": f"Invalid JSON from bridge: {exc}"}
   324|        except Exception as exc:
   325|            return {"ok": False, "error": str(exc)}

{"content": "     1|# Task Schema
     2|
     3|A **Task** is one atomic unit of paralegal work. Each task is represented as a single GitHub Issue in this repo. The issue is the queue entry, the audit log, and the human-in-the-loop touch point all at once.
     4|
     5|## Identity
     6|
     7|Each task issue has:
     8|
     9|| Element | Where it lives | Required | Notes |
    10||---|---|---|---|
    11|| **Issue title** | Issue title | yes | Format: `[<phase>] <case-slug>: <skill> — <short summary>` |
    12|| **Issue body** | First post | yes | YAML frontmatter (see below) + a markdown body |
    13|| **Labels** | Issue labels | yes | See "Label vocabulary" |
    14|| **Comments** | Issue comments | no | Used by workers to log progress and by humans to give instructions |
    15|| **Linked PR** | `Closes` reference | no | If the task produced a PR (e.g. drafting a document) |
    16|
    17|## Issue body frontmatter (canonical)
    18|
    19|```yaml
    20|---
    21|task_id: <stable slug, e.g. dest-adkins-demand-draft-001>
    22|case_slug: destiny-adkins
    23|phase: phase_3_demand
    24|landmark: demand_drafted              # the PHASE_DAG.yaml landmark this task aims to satisfy
    25|skill: demand-letter-generation       # the Skills/<slug>/SKILL.md to load
    26|template: task_templates/draft-demand.yaml  # the task template that produced this issue
    27|status: ready                         # ready | claimed | in_progress | needs_review | done | failed | blocked
    28|priority: normal                      # high | normal | low
    29|created_at: "2026-04-07T13:00:00Z"
    30|created_by: materializer              # materializer | human
    31|depends_on: []                        # list of other task_ids that must be done first
    32|inputs:                               # task-specific inputs the worker needs
    33|  case_file: cases/destiny-adkins/destiny-adkins.md
    34|  due_by: "2026-05-01"
    35|success_check: |                      # a vault predicate that becomes true when this task is done
    36|  case.has_document("demand")
    37|---
    38|```
    39|
    40|## Label vocabulary
    41|
    42|**Routing labels** (every task should have one of each):
    43|
    44|- `case:<slug>` — which matter
    45|- `phase:<phase_n_name>` — which phase per `PHASE_DAG.yaml`
    46|- `skill:<slug>` — which skill should run it
    47|- `status:<lifecycle>` — see below
    48|- `priority:<high|normal|low>`
    49|
    50|**Status labels** (exactly one):
    51|
    52|- `status:ready` — the worker may pick this up
    53|- `status:claimed` — a worker has begun (used to prevent double-pickup)
    54|- `status:in_progress` — actively running
    55|- `status:needs_review` — worker finished, awaiting human approval before downstream tasks unlock
    56|- `status:done` — closed, success_check satisfied
    57|- `status:failed` — closed, success_check not satisfied; see failure comment
    58|- `status:blocked` — depends_on not yet done, OR external info needed
    59|
    60|**Optional flags**:
    61|
    62|- `auto` — worker may complete and close without human approval (safe deterministic tasks)
    63|- `review` — worker must stop after producing artifact and apply `status:needs_review`
    64|- `recurring` — produced by a recurring template (e.g. bi-weekly check-in) and should not be closed permanently
    65|- `pilot` — only operate on cases with `pilot:true` in case frontmatter (used while bringing the runtime up)
    66|
    67|## Lifecycle
    68|
    69|```
    70|   materializer creates issue  ────►  status:ready (or status:blocked if depends_on unmet)
    71|
    72|         │
    73|         │  (worker triggered by status:ready)
    74|         ▼
    75|
    76|   status:claimed  ─►  status:in_progress  ─► success_check?
    77|                                                  │
    78|                              ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐
    79|                              ▼                   ▼                   ▼
    80|                          true,           true,             false
    81|                          auto label      review label
    82|                              │                 │                   │
    83|                              ▼                 ▼                   ▼
    84|                          status:done   status:needs_review     status:failed
    85|                                                  │
    86|                                          (human flips label)
    87|                                                  ▼
    88|                                            status:done
    89|```
    90|
    91|A task is **closed** only on `status:done` or `status:failed`. `needs_review` is open with the agent waiting on a human label change.
    92|
    93|## Idempotency
    94|
    95|Materializer runs every N minutes. To avoid creating duplicates:
    96|
    97|1. Each task template has a `task_id_template` that produces a deterministic id from `(case_slug, landmark, optional discriminator)`.
    98|2. Before opening an issue, the materializer searches for an open issue with the same `task_id` in its frontmatter. If one exists, it skips.
    99|3. For recurring templates, the discriminator is the time bucket (e.g. ISO week) so the next bucket gets a new id.
   100|
   101|## Dependencies
   102|
   103|Tasks express ordering with `depends_on: [task_id, …]`. The materializer:
   104|
   105|- Will not flip a `status:blocked` task to `status:ready` until every `depends_on` task is `status:done`.
   106|- Will close a `status:blocked` task as `status:failed` if any `depends_on` task closes as `status:failed`, with a comment explaining why.
   107|
   108|GitHub's native `blocks #N` / `blocked by #N` keywords can be used in addition for the GitHub UI's benefit, but the runtime authoritatively reads `depends_on` from the task frontmatter.
   109|
   110|## Worker outputs
   111|
   112|When a worker runs a task it should:
   113|
   114|1. Comment on the issue with a one-paragraph "what I did" summary.
   115|2. Commit any vault changes in a single commit whose message references the task: `task <task_id>: <summary>`.
   116|3. Re-run the `success_check` predicate; flip status accordingly.
   117|4. If `status:needs_review`, leave a checklist in the comment of what the human should verify.
   118|5. Never close the issue without first either marking `status:done` or `status:failed`.
   119|
   120|## Failure handling
   121|
   122|A failed task:
   123|
   124|1. Stays open with `status:failed`.
   125|2. Comment explains the failure (error message, what was attempted, what's missing).

---

FIRMVAULT + GSD ARCHITECTURE ANALYSIS

1. FIRMVAULT: CASE PIPELINE SYSTEM

Data Model:
- "The firm as a code repo" - entire law practice state stored as markdown in a Git repo
- Each case lives at cases/<slug>/<slug>.md with YAML frontmatter (case_id, client_name, case_type, status, date_of_incident, landmarks)
- Sub-structure per case: claims/, liens/, contacts/, documents/, Activity Log/
- Master contacts at Contacts/{Clients,Insurance,Medical,Legal}/
- 96 firm templates at Templates/ (LOR, complaints, demands, lien letters)
- PHI never stored - only {{placeholder}} tokens resolved at render time
- 117 real cases currently imported and backfilled
- 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format

Workflow States (PHASE_DAG.yaml - 9 phases):
Phase 0: Onboarding (3 hard blockers: client info, contract, HIPAA)
Phase 1: File Setup (4 soft blockers: intake, accident report, claims, providers)
Phase 2: Treatment (recurring check-ins, records/bills requests, until MMI)
Phase 3: Demand (draft, attorney review, send - demand_sent is hard blocker)
Phase 4: Negotiation (followup, offers, branches to settlement OR litigation)
Phase 5: Settlement (statement, authorization, release, funds, distribution)
Phase 6: Lien Resolution (identify, request final amounts, negotiate, pay)
Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, service, discovery, mediation, trial/settlement)
Phase 8: Closed (terminal)

Case movement: Each phase has landmarks with vault-evaluable predicates. A materializer reads PHASE_DAG + case frontmatter, identifies unsatisfied landmarks, and emits GitHub Issues as tasks. Tasks have lifecycle: ready->claimed->in_progress->needs_review->done/failed. Workers load skills, execute, commit vault changes, PR for review. Transitions happen when exit_blockers (hard/soft) are satisfied.

The _Case Pipeline.md is an Obsidian kanban board showing all ~120 cases distributed across phases.

2. GSD: PROJECT MANAGEMENT FRAMEWORK

Lifecycle: IDEA -> DISCUSS -> RESEARCH -> PLAN -> APPROVE -> EXECUTE -> VERIFY -> SHIP

Data Structure per project (15 projects under /opt/data/projects/):
  .planning/PROJECT.md - vision, constraints, success criteria, stakeholders
  .planning/REQUIREMENTS.md - versioned requirements with acceptance criteria
  .planning/ROADMAP.md - phases with dependencies, effort estimates, goal-backward statements
  .planning/STATE.md - current phase, wave, lifecycle stage, task statuses, decisions, blockers, metrics
  .planning/phases/<phase>/PLAN.md - XML task definitions with assignees, waves, verification

Task model: XML <task> elements in PLAN.md with attributes: id, type (code/document/research/outreach/marketing/operations/review), assignee, wave. Tags: title, description, approval, verify, done.

Task statuses: planned -> dispatched -> in_progress -> blocked -> done -> verified

Wave execution: Tasks in same wave run in parallel. Wave N+1 waits for wave N completion.

Agent routing (.planning/config.json):
- hermes-ceo/hermes -> direct execution
- openclaw/main, openclaw/coder -> relay server
- COO, Marketing-Director, Intake-Specialist, Follow-up-Agent -> Paperclip agents (currently disabled)
- Task types route to specific agent pools

Node.js library (gsd-lawyerinc/src/) provides: parser.js (PLAN.md -> structured tasks), state.js (STATE.md read/write/update), dispatcher.js (route tasks to platforms), mc-sync.js (Mission Control integration)

3. MISSION CONTROL INTEGRATION

GSD -> MC:
- mc-sync.js maps GSD statuses to MC pipeline: planned->inbox, dispatched->assigned, in_progress->in_progress, reviewing->quality_review, done->done
- syncPlanToMC() bulk-creates MC tasks from parsed PLAN.md
- syncStatusToMC() pushes STATE.md changes to MC API
- Task mappings persisted in .planning/mc-mappings.json
- MC at ops.lawyerincorporated.com, authenticated via x-api-key header
- Each project gets an MC project (mission_control_project_id in PROJECT.md frontmatter)

FirmVault -> MC:
- FirmVault's DESIGN.md evaluates MC as one of 5 candidate architectures (Arch 3)
- MC would serve as task store + Aegis quality reviewer + 32-panel dashboard
- FirmVault materializer would create MC tasks from landmark gaps
- Not yet wired - still in architecture bake-off phase

Daemon integration:
- Python adapter (daemon/adapters/gsd.py) implements TaskSource + HealthCheckable interfaces
- Shells out to Node.js bridge (daemon/gsd_bridge.mjs) which imports gsd-lawyerinc library
- On each daemon tick: discovers projects, reads STATE.md, checks wave completion, dispatches next wave, flags approval-needed tasks for Telegram notification
- Task IDs formatted as "project_name:TASK-ID"
- Bridge commands: health_check, poll_project, dispatch_wave, update_status, list_projects

4. OVERLAPS BETWEEN SYSTEMS

Conceptual parallels:
- FirmVault's PHASE_DAG phases <-> GSD's lifecycle stages (both are state machines with gates)
- FirmVault's landmarks <-> GSD's "Must Be True" conditions (goal-backward design)
- FirmVault's materializer + task templates <-> GSD's PLAN.md + parser
- FirmVault's worker + skill <-> GSD's dispatcher + agent routing
- FirmVault's task schema (ready->claimed->in_progress->done) <-> GSD's task statuses (planned->dispatched->in_progress->done)
- Both use frontmatter-rich markdown as state store
- Both commit to "vault as truth" philosophy
- Both have wave/dependency ordering concepts

Key differences:
- FirmVault is CASE-centric (legal matters with domain-specific phases). GSD is PROJECT-centric (software/business projects with generic lifecycle)
- FirmVault uses vault-evaluable predicates for progression. GSD uses human/agent judgment
- FirmVault tasks are GitHub Issues. GSD tasks are XML in PLAN.md files, dispatched to agents
- FirmVault has 42 domain-specific skills (lien-management, deposition-prep). GSD routes generic task types (code, research, outreach)
- FirmVault's PHASE_DAG is a fixed 9-phase legal workflow. GSD's roadmap phases are custom per project
- GSD has active MC sync and multi-agent dispatch working. FirmVault's runtime is still document-stage

Shared infrastructure:
- Both target Mission Control as the operational dashboard
- Both use the Hermes daemon as their execution engine
- Both share the same agent pool (hermes-ceo, openclaw, Paperclip agents)
- GSD manages FirmVault-adjacent work - one of the 15 projects is literally "whaley-law-firm"

The systems are complementary: GSD manages the meta-work of building and improving the firm's infrastructure, while FirmVault manages the actual legal casework the firm does for clients.
